Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively

Memory.value: When memories come to mind of what happened, I feel my body is in the same position as when the frightening event occurred.
When I think about the frightening event I can sometimes smell things that I smelt when the frightening event happened.
Remembering what happened during the frightening event is just like looking at photographs of it in my mind.

Negative Appraisals.value: I will not be able to control my anger and will do something terrible
I feel isolated and set apart from others
Nothing good can happen to me anymore

issues.explanation: Widowed is implausible for age 29 under the validation rules (widowed is implausible if age < 22 only).
Widowed is implausible at age 29 under the validation rules.

vignette:
The patient is a 29-year-old non-binary person from Oceania who is currently in a relationship and presents with severe post-traumatic stress symptoms following a violent incident. Their distress is persistent and broad-based, with a PCL-5 score of 70 indicating marked symptom burden. In daily life, they are particularly unsettled by crowded places, crying, and the sight or sound of objects being thrown, all of which can rapidly pull them into a state of alarm. At those times they often cover their face with their hands, try to mentally redirect themselves, or leave the site of the event and avoid returning.

When reminders arise, the experience is not simply remembered but relived. They describe bodily reenactment in which their body feels positioned as it was during the frightening event, alongside vivid photographic images of what happened. Smells from the original incident can also return with striking immediacy, intensifying the sense that the event is happening again. These memory experiences are closely tied to their sense of present danger and are accompanied by strong physical reactions, difficulty concentrating, and irritability with angry outbursts that can become aggressive.

Their negative appraisals are prominent and pessimistic. They worry that they will not be able to control their anger and will do something terrible, and they feel isolated and set apart from other people. They also hold a bleak view of the future, with a sense that nothing good can happen to them anymore. These beliefs sit alongside frequent internal alarm and keep their threat system highly activated.

In turn, the intrusive memory experiences make it harder for them to settle, and they become more vigilant to crowded environments, crying, and thrown objects. This heightened state of threat then feeds further attempts to hide, avoid, or mentally redirect away from distressing material. Although these strategies offer short-term relief, they appear to narrow their life further and interfere with connection, including within their relationship, leaving them increasingly cut off and strained by the ongoing impact of the trauma.
